11.title: The accusative: nominative → object form
11.intro: The accusative marks the direct object — the thing receiving the action ('I drink COFFEE'). The rules are tiny: feminine -a becomes -u, masculine inanimate and neuter nouns don't change at all, and masculine ANIMATE nouns (people, animals) add -a. Learn these real transformation pairs.

items.note: Feminine -a → -u: Pijem kavu. (I drink coffee.)
Pijem vodu. (I drink water.)
Čitam knjigu. (I am reading a book.)
Jedem ribu. (I am eating fish.)
Volim Hrvatsku. (I love Croatia.) Country names in -a follow the same rule.
Masculine INANIMATE stays the same: Vidim grad. (I see the city.)
Jedem kruh. (I am eating bread.) No change.
Pijem sok. (I drink juice.) No change.
Neuter (-o/-e) never changes: Pijem pivo.
Masculine ANIMATE adds -a: Vidim prijatelja. (I see a friend.)
Animate: Imam brata. (I have a brother.)
Animate + 'fleeting a': the a of pas drops → Imam psa. (I have a dog.)

11.title: Accusative practice: I + verb + object
11.intro: Turn nouns into their object form and build real 'I + verb + object' sentences.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I drink coffee'?
questions.explanation: kava is feminine, so as the direct object -a changes to -u: Pijem kavu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the accusative of 'knjiga': Čitam ___ .
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -u: knjiga → knjigu. Čitam knjigu = I am reading a book.

questions.prompt: Which noun does NOT change in the accusative?
questions.explanation: grad is masculine inanimate, so the accusative looks exactly like the nominative: Vidim grad.

questions.prompt: Complete with the accusative of 'voda': Pijem ___ .
questions.explanation: voda → vodu (feminine -a → -u).

questions.prompt: Complete: Vidim ___ . ('I see my brother')
questions.explanation: brat is masculine ANIMATE, so the accusative adds -a: Vidim brata.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'The woman drinks coffee.'
questions.explanation: Žena (subject, nominative) pije kavu (object, accusative).

questions.prompt: Complete: Volim ___ . ('I love Croatia' — Hrvatska)
questions.explanation: Hrvatska ends in -a, so the object form is Hrvatsku: Volim Hrvatsku.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I am eating a sandwich'?
questions.explanation: sendvič is masculine inanimate — it stays the same in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Complete: Imam ___ . ('I have a dog' — pas)
questions.explanation: pas is animate (add -a) AND has a fleeting a that drops: pas → psa. Imam psa.

12.title: Saying no and asking yes/no questions
12.intro: Negation is beautifully simple: put 'ne' directly before the verb. Only imati fuses into one word (nemam). For yes/no questions, put 'li' right after the verb: Govoriš li engleski? Master these patterns and your range doubles.

items.note: ne + verb, always directly before the verb.
Your most useful survival sentence.
ne + verb + accusative object.
Only imati fuses with ne: nemam, NEVER 'ne imam'.
biti also has fused negatives: nisam, nisi, nije… (day 6 review)
Yes/no question: verb + li + rest.
vi-form for strangers and groups.
'Da li…?' exists too, but 'Govoriš li…?' is the preferred standard form in Croatia.
Answering negatively doubles the ne: 'Ne,' (no) + 'ne govorim' (I don't speak).

12.title: Negate it, question it
12.intro: Practise 'ne' + verb, the fused 'nemam', and building li-questions.

questions.prompt: Make it negative: 'Govorim engleski.'
questions.explanation: ne stands directly before the verb, as a separate word.

questions.prompt: Say 'I don't understand.'
questions.explanation: ne + razumijem. The single most useful phrase for a learner.

questions.prompt: What is the negative of 'imam' (I have)?
questions.explanation: imati is the only verb that fuses with ne: nemam, nemaš, nema… 'ne imam' is wrong.

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'Do you speak English?'
questions.explanation: Yes/no question = verb first, then li, then the rest.

questions.prompt: Where does 'li' go in a yes/no question?
questions.explanation: li always comes immediately after the verb — never first, never last.

questions.prompt: Ask a friend 'Do you drink coffee?' (piti, informal)
questions.explanation: piješ (you drink) + li + kavu (accusative object).

questions.prompt: Which question means the same as 'Govoriš li engleski?'
questions.explanation: 'Da li + verb' is an alternative yes/no question form; in Croatia 'verb + li' is preferred in the standard language.

questions.prompt: Answer 'Radiš li danas?' with NO:
questions.explanation: First 'Ne,' answers the question; then 'ne radim' negates the verb. Croatian happily doubles the ne.

13.title: The seven question words (+ two bonus ones)
13.intro: Seven little words open every information question in Croatian: tko, što, gdje, kada, zašto, kako, koliko. Learn each one inside a real question you will actually ask.

items.note: Tko je to? — Who is that?
Što radiš? — What are you doing?
Gdje je kolodvor? — Where is the station?
Kada radiš? — When do you work? (also shortened: kad)
Zašto učiš hrvatski? — Why are you learning Croatian?
Kako si? — How are you?
Koliko košta? — How much does it cost?
Bonus: Odakle si? — Where are you from?

items.en: What is your name? (literally: how do you call yourself)
items.note: Croatian asks 'how', not 'what', for names.
Survival question number one when travelling.

13.title: Ask the right question
13.intro: Match each situation to the right question word and build full questions.

questions.prompt: Which word means 'where'?
questions.explanation: gdje = where. Gdje je kolodvor? — Where is the station?

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ košta kava? (How much does the coffee cost?)
questions.explanation: koliko = how much/how many. Don't mix it up with kako (how).

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ se zoveš? (What's your name?)
questions.explanation: Croatian literally asks 'HOW do you call yourself': Kako se zoveš?

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ je to? — To je Marko. (…is that? — That's Marko.)
questions.explanation: The answer is a person, so the question word is tko (who). For things you would use što (what).

questions.prompt: Ask 'Where is the station?': ___ je kolodvor?
questions.explanation: gdje = where. Gdje je kolodvor?

questions.prompt: Which word means 'why'?
questions.explanation: zašto = why. Careful: zato = 'that's why / because of that' — the answer, not the question!

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'How much does the beer cost?'
questions.explanation: Question word first: Koliko košta pivo?

questions.prompt: What does 'Što radiš?' mean?
questions.explanation: što = what, radiš = you do/are doing. A very common conversation opener.

14.title: Your A0 toolkit — everything in one place
14.intro: Milestone day! These ten lines pack together everything from days 1-13: greetings, introducing yourself, biti, verbs, the accusative, negation and questions. If you can produce all ten from memory, you have reached A0.

items.note: Greeting + biti (day 2 + 6).

items.hr: Zovem se Alex. Drago mi je.
items.en: My name is Alex. Nice to meet you.
items.note: The polite full introduction (day 3).

items.hr: Odakle si? — Ja sam iz Amerike.
items.en: Where are you from? — I am from America.
items.note: 'iz + place' = from. Fixed phrase for now.
Present tense endings (day 8).
Politeness + negation (day 12).
One magic word when you miss something.

items.en: How much does it cost? — Ten euros.
items.note: Question word + numbers (days 7 + 13).

items.hr: Pijem kavu, a on pije pivo.
items.en: I drink coffee, and he drinks beer.
items.note: Nominative subject + accusative object in action (days 10-11).
li-question (day 12).
Closing any interaction politely (day 2).

14.title: A0 milestone quiz
14.intro: A mixed check across everything so far: greetings, biti, genders, verbs, accusative, negation and questions. Aim for at least 8 out of 10.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'thank you'?
questions.explanation: hvala = thanks; molim = please / you're welcome.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ___ student. (I am a student.)
questions.explanation: Short form of biti: ja sam, ti si, on je…

questions.prompt: What gender is 'kava'?
questions.explanation: Nouns in -a are (almost always) feminine: kava, žena, škola.

questions.prompt: Conjugate: Mi ___ hrvatski. (govoriti — we speak)
questions.explanation: mi-form ends in -mo: govorimo.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'She is not here'?
questions.explanation: biti has fused negatives: nije = is not. 'ne je' does not exist.

questions.prompt: Complete with the object form: Pijem ___ . (kava)
questions.explanation: Accusative of kava is kavu — feminine -a → -u.

questions.prompt: Which number is 'petnaest'?
questions.explanation: pet = 5, petnaest = 15, pedeset = 50. The -naest numbers are the teens.

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'What is your name?'
questions.explanation: Kako se zoveš? — literally 'How do you call yourself?'

questions.prompt: Ask 'Do you speak English?'
questions.explanation: Yes/no question: verb + li. (Without li it can work in speech, but the li-form is the one to master.)

questions.prompt: Say 'I don't understand.'
questions.explanation: ne + razumijem — negation goes directly before the verb.

14.title: Milestone conversation: meeting Ivana
14.intro: Your first full conversation in Croatian — a real exchange using only what you've learned in the first two weeks. Read your lines ('Vi') aloud; better yet, cover the English and check yourself.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Ja sam Ivana. Kako se zovete?
lines.en: Good day! I am Ivana. What is your name? (formal)

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Zovem se Alex. Drago mi je.
lines.en: Good day! My name is Alex. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Nice to meet you. Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Ja sam iz Amerike. Sada živim u Zagrebu.
lines.en: I am from America. Now I live in Zagreb.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you speak Croatian?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Govorim malo hrvatski. Učim svaki dan.
lines.en: I speak a little Croatian. I study every day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Excellent! Do you drink coffee?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Da, volim kavu. Pijem kavu svaki dan.
lines.en: Yes, I love coffee. I drink coffee every day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Great! Goodbye, Alex!

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.en: Goodbye, Ivana! Thanks!

15.title: imati — to have (and its special negative)
15.intro: imati is a perfectly regular -am verb, and it pairs beautifully with the accusative you learned on day 11: Imam brata, Imam psa. Its negative is special — it fuses into one word: nemam, never 'ne imam'.

items.note: Imam auto. — I have a car.
Imaš li vremena? — Do you have time? (fixed phrase)
Ona ima sestru. — She has a sister.
Imamo kuću i vrt. — We have a house and a garden.
Imate li djecu? — Do you have children?
Oni imaju psa. — They have a dog.
The ONLY verb that fuses with ne: nemam, nemaš, nema, nemamo, nemate, nemaju.

items.en: I have a brother and a sister.
items.note: Accusative in action: brat → brata (animate), sestra → sestru (fem. -a → -u).
imati + li question. auto is masculine inanimate — no change.
pas → psa: animate -a plus the fleeting a drops.

items.en: I don't have a car, but I have a bicycle.
items.note: ali = but. Contrast two possessions in one sentence.
Everyday set phrase — you'll hear it constantly. (Literally 'there is no problem.')

15.title: What do you have?
15.intro: Conjugate imati, use its fused negative, and combine it with accusative objects.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ___ brata. (I have a brother.)
questions.explanation: imati is a regular -am verb: imam, imaš, ima…

questions.prompt: What is the negative of 'imam'?
questions.explanation: imati fuses with ne into nemati: nemam, nemaš, nema…

questions.prompt: Ask a friend: ___ li auto? (Do you have a car?)
questions.explanation: ti-form imaš + li: Imaš li auto?

questions.prompt: Complete: On ___ psa. (He has a dog.)
questions.explanation: on/ona/ono → ima (no ending). psa is the accusative of pas.

questions.prompt: Complete: Mi ___ kuću. (We have a house.)
questions.explanation: mi-form ends in -mo: imamo. kuća → kuću (accusative).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I have a sister'?
questions.explanation: sestra is the object → accusative sestru (feminine -a → -u).

questions.prompt: Build the polite question: 'Do you have children?'
questions.explanation: Formal imate + li + djecu (accusative of djeca, children).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'We don't have a dog'?
questions.explanation: Fused negative nemamo + accusative psa. 'Ne imamo' is wrong, and pas must change to psa.

16.title: Ten verbs that carry your day
16.intro: These ten high-frequency verbs cover most of what you do daily. For each one, learn the ja-form — the rest follows the regular endings you know (-m, -š, –, -mo, -te, -ju/-e). Watch out: a few have a present stem different from the infinitive.

items.note: idem, ideš, ide, idemo, idete, idu. Idem u grad. (u + accusative = motion 'into')
znam, znaš, zna, znamo, znate, znaju. Znam malo hrvatski.
vidim, vidiš, vidi, vidimo, vidite, vide. Vidim more. (I see the sea.)
Irregular stem: jedem, jedeš, jede, jedemo, jedete, jedu. Jedem sendvič.
pijem, piješ, pije, pijemo, pijete, piju. Pijem vodu.
Regular -am verb. Gledam televiziju.
Slušam glazbu. (I listen to music. glazba → glazbu, accusative)
učim, učiš, uči… Učim hrvatski svaki dan.
Your day-8 model verb: radim, radiš, radi, radimo, radite, rade.
-ovati verbs switch to -ujem: kupujem, kupuješ… Kupujem kruh.

16.title: Conjugation workout
16.intro: Put the right ending on the right stem — including the tricky irregular stems.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ___ u grad. (ići — I am going to town.)
questions.explanation: ići has the present stem id-: idem, ideš, ide…

questions.prompt: Complete: Oni ___ hrvatski. (znati — they know)
questions.explanation: -am verbs take -aju in the oni-form: znaju.

questions.prompt: Complete: Mi ___ more. (vidjeti — we see the sea.)
questions.explanation: vidjeti conjugates like an -im verb: vidim, vidiš… vidimo.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I eat bread'?
questions.explanation: jesti has the irregular stem jed-: jedem. kruh is masculine inanimate — unchanged in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ti ___ kavu. (piti — you drink coffee.)
questions.explanation: piti → pijem, piješ (with j and the š ending for ti).

questions.prompt: What is the ja-form of 'kupovati' (to buy)?
questions.explanation: Verbs in -ovati switch to -ujem in the present: kupovati → kupujem.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I am going to town.'
questions.explanation: u + accusative expresses motion into a place; grad doesn't change (masc. inanimate).

questions.prompt: Complete: Oni ___ televiziju. (gledati — they watch TV.)
questions.explanation: gledati is a regular -am verb: oni gledaju. televizija → televiziju (accusative).

17.title: htjeti & moći — wanting and being able
17.intro: These two irregular verbs plus an infinitive unlock an enormous range: wanting, asking, requesting, refusing. Note the odd endings: hoću/mogu end in -u, and 'Mogu li…?' is your polite-request machine.

items.note: hoću, hoćeš, hoće, hoćemo, hoćete, hoće. Irregular!
Fused negative: neću, nećeš, neće, nećemo, nećete, neće.
mogu, možeš, može, možemo, možete, mogu. The ja- and oni-forms end in -u!

items.en: Do you want to go to the cinema?
items.note: htjeti + li + infinitive. Infinitives end in -ti or -ći and never change.

items.en: I do! / Yes, I want to!
items.note: A one-word enthusiastic yes.
THE polite request pattern: Mogu li dobiti + accusative.
Survival phrase for every conversation.
Learn it as a fixed phrase — sporije = more slowly.
ne + mogu + infinitive. doći = to come.
željeti (želim) is a softer, politer 'want' than hoću — perfect for ordering too.

items.en: Shall we go to the seaside?
items.note: hoćemo li = shall we. na + accusative = motion 'onto/to'.

17.title: Want it, request it
17.intro: Drill the irregular forms of htjeti and moći and build polite requests with an infinitive.

questions.prompt: What is the ja-form of 'htjeti' (to want)?
questions.explanation: htjeti is irregular: hoću, hoćeš, hoće, hoćemo, hoćete, hoće.

questions.prompt: What is the ja-form of 'moći' (can)?
questions.explanation: mogu ends in -u — a rare ending shared only with hoću. 'možem' is a common learner error.

questions.prompt: Ask a friend: ___ li ponoviti? (Can you repeat?)
questions.explanation: ti-form of moći is možeš: Možeš li ponoviti?

questions.prompt: Complete: Ne ___ doći. (I can't come.)
questions.explanation: ne + mogu + infinitive: Ne mogu doći.

questions.prompt: What is the fused negative of 'hoću'?
questions.explanation: htjeti fuses with ne: neću, nećeš, neće… ('ne hoću' is wrong.)

questions.prompt: Build the polite request: 'Can I get a coffee?'
questions.explanation: Mogu + li + infinitive + accusative object — your all-purpose request pattern.

questions.prompt: 'Hoćeš li ići u kino?' — pick the enthusiastic YES:
questions.explanation: Answer a htjeti-question with the same verb: Hoću! (I want to!) / Neću. (I don't want to.)

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ___ učiti hrvatski. (željeti — I want to study Croatian.)
questions.explanation: željeti → želim; a softer 'want' than hoću, and the verb you'll use to order food tomorrow.

18.title: The menu: what you'll eat and drink
18.intro: Real café and konoba vocabulary. Every noun comes with its accusative — the form you actually say when ordering, because what you order is always a direct object.

items.note: Order: jednu kavu (one coffee). Croatia runs on coffee.
Masculine — unchanged: Želim čaj.
sok od naranče = orange juice. Unchanged: Pijem sok.
Order: vodu. Mineralna voda = mineral water.
Neuter — unchanged: jedno pivo, molim.
bijelo vino (white), crno vino (red — literally 'black').
Unchanged: Jedem kruh.
Unchanged: Želim sir.
Order: juhu (feminine -a → -u).
Order: salatu.
Order: ribu. Essential on the coast!
Unchanged: Želim sladoled.
Usually plural: Želim palačinke. A Croatian dessert classic.

18.title: Ordering like a local
18.intro: Ten phrases carry the entire café interaction — ordering, asking the price, paying and toasting. Combine them with the menu words and the accusative from day 11 and you can survive any café in Croatia.

items.note: Molim vas + accusative = the simplest polite order.

items.en: I would like a pizza, please.
items.note: želim + accusative — politer than hoću when ordering.
za mene = for me. Handy when ordering in a group.
jelovnik = menu. Yesterday's request pattern in action.
Koliko košta kava? — How much is the coffee?
Short and universal. Also: Mogu li dobiti račun?

items.en: Here you are. / What would you like?
items.note: The waiter's word — both when serving you and when inviting your order.
Answer: Ne, hvala. (No, thanks.) or Da, još jednu kavu. (Yes, one more coffee.)
Said before eating — the Croatian 'bon appétit'.
The toast — literally 'may you live long'.

18.title: Order a three-course interaction
18.intro: From 'Izvolite?' to the bill — practise every step of ordering.

questions.prompt: Order one coffee politely:
questions.explanation: Molim vas + jednu kavu (accusative). The others are rude or ungrammatical.

questions.prompt: Complete your order: Želim ___ , molim. (pizza)
questions.explanation: pizza is feminine → accusative pizzu: Želim pizzu, molim.

questions.prompt: How do you ask 'How much does it cost?'
questions.explanation: koliko = how much. (Koliko je sati? asks the TIME, not the price.)

questions.prompt: Ask for the bill: ___ , molim.
questions.explanation: Račun, molim. — The bill, please. Short and always understood.

questions.prompt: The waiter says 'Izvolite.' What does it mean?
options: Here you are. / What would you like?
questions.answer: Here you are. / What would you like?
questions.explanation: Izvolite is the all-purpose service word — inviting your order or handing you something.

questions.prompt: Build the request: 'Can I get the menu?'
questions.explanation: Mogu li dobiti + object. jelovnik is masculine inanimate — unchanged.

questions.prompt: What do you say before people start eating?
questions.explanation: Dobar tek! = enjoy your meal. Živjeli! is the toast for drinks.

questions.prompt: You raise your glass. What do you say?
questions.explanation: Živjeli! = Cheers! (literally 'may you live long').

18.title: At the café: a full order, start to finish
18.intro: A complete café scene in Zagreb — greeting, ordering food and drink, asking for the bill, paying. This is the exact interaction you'll have on your first day in Croatia. Play your side ('Vi') aloud.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good day! What would you like?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Molim vas, jednu kavu i vodu.
lines.en: Good day! One coffee and a water, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Naravno. Želite li i nešto jesti?
lines.en: Of course. Would you like something to eat as well?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Da, želim juhu i kruh, molim.
lines.en: Yes, I'd like the soup and bread, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Izvolite — juha, kruh, kava i voda. Dobar tek!
lines.en: Here you are — soup, bread, coffee and water. Enjoy your meal!

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.en: Thank you very much!

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.en: Excuse me, can I get the bill?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Of course. Eight euros altogether.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.en: Here you are. Thanks and goodbye!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Thank YOU! Goodbye!

19.title: Numbers 20-100: one simple pattern
19.intro: The tens are all built the same way: number + -deset. Then compounds just stack: dvadeset pet = 25. Learn the nine tens and you can say any price, age or phone number up to 100.

items.note: dva + deset. Careful: dvanaest = 12!
tri + deset.
Slightly shortened: četrdeset, not 'četirideset'.
Shortened from pet: PEdeset, not 'petdeset'.
Shortened from šest: šeZdeset.
sedam + deset — fully regular.
osam + deset.
devet loses its t: deveDEset.
Just sto — no 'jedan' needed.
Compounds simply stack: trideset dva (32), pedeset sedam (57), devedeset devet (99).

19.title: Telling the time & saying prices
19.intro: Time and prices use one micro-rule: after 1 say sat, after 2-4 say sata, after 5+ say sati (and euro behaves the same: dva eura, pet eura). Plus the famous trap: 'pola osam' means HALF TO eight = 7:30.

items.note: Literally 'how many of hours is it?'
After 1 → sat.
After 2, 3, 4 → sata: dva sata, tri sata, četiri sata.
After 5 and up → sati: pet sati, deset sati, jedanaest sati.
hour + i + minutes: osam i dvadeset = 8:20.
The trap! pola + the NEXT hour: pola osam = 7:30, pola devet = 8:30.
U koliko sati radiš? — At what time do you work?
u + time = at: U osam sati sam u školi.
After 2-4 → eura: dva eura, tri eura.
After 5+ (and compound numbers ending in 5+) → eura as well: pet eura, dvadeset pet eura.

19.title: Prices, times, phone numbers
19.intro: Read numbers, tell the time and handle prices — the numbers you'll actually meet every day.

questions.prompt: Which is 40?
questions.explanation: 40 = četrdeset (shortened). četrnaest = 14.

questions.prompt: Write 60 in Croatian:
questions.explanation: šest shortens and the s voices to z before d: šezdeset (not 'šestdeset').

questions.prompt: Which is 75?
questions.explanation: 70 = sedamdeset, then stack the 5: sedamdeset pet. (pedeset sedam = 57!)

questions.prompt: Write 100 in Croatian:
questions.explanation: 100 = sto, with no 'jedan' in front.

questions.prompt: How do you ask 'What time is it?'
questions.explanation: Koliko je sati? = What time is it? (Koliko košta? asks the price.)

questions.prompt: It's 7:30. What do you say?
questions.explanation: pola + the NEXT hour: pola osam = half of the eighth hour = 7:30.

questions.prompt: Complete: Tri su ___ . (It's three o'clock.)
questions.explanation: After 2, 3 and 4 the word for hour is sata: tri sata.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'at eight o'clock'?
questions.explanation: u + time = at: u osam sati. After 5+ the form is sati.

questions.prompt: Complete the price: Kava košta dva ___ .
questions.explanation: After 2, 3, 4 the noun takes the -a form: dva eura, tri eura. (jedan euro, pet eura.)

20.title: Ten adjectives in three genders
20.intro: Adjectives copy the gender of their noun: masculine ends in a consonant (or -i), feminine in -a, neuter in -o. Learn each adjective as a trio — velik grad, velika kuća, veliko selo — and agreement becomes automatic.

items.note: Fleeting a: dobAr but dobra, dobro. dobar dan, dobra kava, dobro pivo.
velik grad, velika kuća, veliko selo.
mali stan, mala kuća, malo selo.
lijep grad, lijepa žena, lijepo more.
nov auto? Careful: auto is MASCULINE despite the -o: nov auto, nova knjiga, novo selo.
star grad, stara škola, staro vino.
mlad čovjek, mlada žena.
brz auto, brza žena, brzo pitanje. (brzo alone also = 'quickly')
skup restoran, skupa kava, skupo vino.
jeftin stan, jeftina pizza, jeftino pivo.

20.title: Make it agree
20.intro: Pick or produce the adjective ending that matches the noun's gender.

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ kuća (a big house)
questions.explanation: kuća is feminine → the adjective takes -a: velika kuća.

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ grad (a small city)
questions.explanation: grad is masculine → mali grad.

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ pivo (a good beer)
questions.explanation: pivo is neuter (-o) → dobro pivo. Note dobar loses its a: dobar → dobro.

questions.prompt: Complete: Kava je ___ . (The coffee is expensive.)
questions.explanation: kava is feminine → skupa: Kava je skupa.

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ more (a beautiful sea)
questions.explanation: more is neuter (-e) → lijepo more.

questions.prompt: Complete: Auto je ___ . (The car is new.)
questions.explanation: Trap! auto ends in -o but is MASCULINE, so: Auto je nov.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'My city is big.'
questions.explanation: Possessive + noun + je + adjective — everything masculine to match grad.

questions.prompt: Which phrase is correct?
questions.explanation: žena is feminine → the adjective must end in -a: stara žena (an old woman).

20.title: Describing your city and home
20.intro: A natural chat that puts today's adjectives to work — describing your city, your home and agreeing adjectives on the fly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: What is your city like?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Moj grad je velik i lijep.
lines.en: My city is big and beautiful.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Do you have a big house?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Ne, imam mali stan. Ali stan je nov.
lines.en: No, I have a small flat. But the flat is new.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Is the flat expensive?

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.hr: Nije, stan je jeftin. A tvoj stan?
lines.en: It isn't, the flat is cheap. And your flat?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Moj stan je star, ali je velik.
lines.en: My flat is old, but it's big.

lines.speaker: Vi
lines.en: Great! Old flats are beautiful.
